name: Playwright
scope: A person with BASIC proficiency level in Playwright as a competency should be able to explain what Playwright is, its core concepts (Browser, BrowserContext, Page, Frame, Locator, Request, Response), and when to use it for web UI and API test automation. They can write simple Playwright tests using Playwright Test in TypeScript or JavaScript, structure tests with describe/test blocks, hooks, and fixtures, and use locators (getByRole, getByText, getByLabel, getByTestId, CSS, XPath) for resilient element targeting. They perform basic user interactions (click, fill, type, select, check, hover, upload, drag and drop), assertions (expect API for visibility, text, value, URL, count, state), and understand auto-waiting, timeouts, and test flakiness. They can handle navigation, multi-page flows, frames/iframes, browser contexts, cookies, storage, and simple authentication. They manage test data and environment config, use environment variables, and prepare/reset state with hooks or provided utilities. They run tests across Chromium, Firefox, WebKit, and device emulations, recognize parallel execution, and handle simple network mocking, API assertions, and request/response waits. They debug with headed mode, slow motion, Playwright Inspector, logs, screenshots, and traces, and distinguish test vs app bugs. They follow best practices for readability, maintainability, and code style, avoid duplication, and use simple abstractions. They understand Playwright project structure, config files, CLI commands, test filtering, tagging, and basic CI integration. They use built-in HTML reports, artifacts (screenshots, videos, traces), and collaborate via code reviews, documentation, and clear communication about failures and workflows.
long_scope:
{"sections": [{"name": "Core Playwright Concepts", "scope_included": "- Understands what Playwright is and when it is appropriate to use for web UI and API test automation
- Understands Playwright’s main building blocks: Browser, BrowserContext, Page, Frame, Locator, Request, Response
- Understands the difference between Playwright Test (the built‑in test runner) and the core Playwright library
- Understands the lifecycle of a typical Playwright test: test startup, browser/context/page creation, test execution, teardown
- Understands headless vs headed execution and when to use each
- Understands that Playwright is cross‑browser and cross‑platform and what that implies for test coverage
- Understands the role of auto‑waiting and how Playwright operations are generally retried until conditions are met or time out
- Understands at a basic level how Playwright integrates into a broader test strategy (unit, API, UI, E2E, regression suites)
- Understands the difference between functional UI tests and non‑functional checks (performance, visual, etc.), even if not yet implementing all of them with Playwright
- Understands what flakiness means in browser tests and how Playwright’s design helps reduce it", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Test Authoring and Structure", "scope_included": "- Can author readable Playwright tests in the primary project language (commonly TypeScript or JavaScript) using Playwright Test
- Uses the test runner’s basic primitives: describe/test blocks, test.step or similar patterns, beforeEach/afterEach hooks for setup and teardown
- Structures tests so that each test case focuses on a clear, single behavior or scenario
- Uses fixtures provided by Playwright Test (such as page, context, browser) correctly at a basic level
- Can group related tests into files and describe blocks to represent features or user flows
- Writes tests that follow the Arrange–Act–Assert pattern in Playwright terms: page setup, user interaction, verification
- Understands the difference between UI tests that span multiple pages and smaller, more focused checks and can choose an appropriate level of granularity
- Can refactor duplicated code within a file into helper functions or minimal abstractions
- Keeps test code separated from production application code while still being able to reference shared utilities where appropriate", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Locators, Interactions and Assertions", "scope_included": "- Understands what a Locator is and why Playwright recommends locators over direct selector usage on the page object
- Uses built‑in locator strategies: getByRole, getByText, getByLabel, getByPlaceholder, getByTestId, CSS selectors, and XPath selectors when necessary
- Chooses locators that are resilient to UI layout changes, preferring semantic and accessibility‑friendly selectors over brittle ones
- Uses chained locators and filters (such as filter by text or nth element) to precisely target elements when needed
- Performs core user interactions: click, fill, type, press, selectOption, check/uncheck, hover, upload files, drag and drop at a basic level
- Uses keyboard and mouse actions for scenarios where standard element methods are not sufficient
- Performs assertions using Playwright Test’s expect API for visibility, text, value, attribute, URL, count, and state (enabled, disabled, checked)
- Uses soft assertions when appropriate and understands the difference from regular assertions
- Uses toHaveURL, toHaveTitle and similar page‑level assertions to verify navigation outcomes
- Understands and uses assertion timeouts and auto‑retry behavior to stabilize tests while avoiding unnecessary sleeps", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Waiting, Timing and Test Reliability", "scope_included": "- Understands Playwright’s auto‑waiting behavior for most locator actions and assertions
- Knows the main wait patterns: waiting for elements to be visible, enabled, attached, or hidden before interacting
- Avoids unconditional fixed sleeps in favor of condition‑based waits, and can replace simple sleep usage with proper waits
- Uses page.waitForURL, waitForNavigation, waitForLoadState at a basic level when navigation or page load events are critical to a test
- Understands the difference between timeout at the test level vs timeout at the action/assertion level
- Can adjust timeouts in a controlled way to handle slow but valid flows without masking underlying issues
- Handles dynamic or delayed content (such as spinners, lazy‑loaded lists, or progressive rendering) using Playwright’s waiting capabilities
- Identifies common causes of flaky tests related to timing and introduces appropriate waits or assertions to address them
- Understands the trade‑off between making tests robust versus making them too slow by over‑waiting", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Navigation, Page Content and Browser Contexts", "scope_included": "- Can open pages and navigate within a web application using page.goto and user interactions such as clicking links and buttons
- Understands and uses BrowserContext to isolate sessions and state between tests at a basic level
- Understands cookies, local storage and session storage conceptually and can inspect or clear them through a context or page
- Handles simple multi‑tab or popup flows by waiting for new pages and switching to them when needed
- Understands frame and iframe concepts and can access elements inside frames when required
- Uses page content inspection capabilities to retrieve text, attributes, or HTML for verification when assertions require it
- Understands the implications of reusing vs recreating contexts or pages between tests on performance and isolation
- Can handle simple authentication flows within tests, using either UI login or stored auth state where guided", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Test Data, State and Environment Handling", "scope_included": "- Understands the importance of deterministic test data and consistent initial state for Playwright tests
- Uses configuration, environment variables or lightweight config files to point tests to different environments (such as staging vs local)
- Can seed or prepare test data via UI steps, simple API calls, or database utilities provided by others
- Uses per‑test or per‑suite setup hooks to create or reset state required by tests (such as user accounts, feature flags or application settings)
- Understands the basics of authenticated vs unauthenticated scenarios and can manage login state within tests at a basic level
- Can use stored authentication state or simple fixtures for sharing login state between tests when provided with patterns
- Understands how to avoid cross‑test dependencies by cleaning up state or using unique test data where practical
- Handles configuration of base URLs, test IDs and other environment‑specific settings through Playwright Test configuration and environment variables", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Cross-Browser, Devices and Parallel Execution", "scope_included": "- Understands that Playwright supports Chromium, Firefox and WebKit and that tests should usually run across multiple browsers
- Can enable and run tests across the main browsers using basic configuration or command‑line options
- Understands the concept of device emulation (viewport size, user agent, touch support) and can run tests with a small set of device profiles where configured
- Recognizes common browser‑specific issues and can help investigate them when tests fail on one browser but not others
- Understands parallel execution at a basic level and can run tests in parallel via Playwright Test settings or CLI flags
- Recognizes when tests are not safe to run in parallel and can mark them appropriately or structure them to avoid interference
- Understands the impact of browser selection, device emulation and parallelism on test duration and resource usage, even if not fully optimizing them", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Network, API Interaction and Mocking (Basic Level)", "scope_included": "- Understands that Playwright can observe and modify network traffic during tests
- Can wait for specific network requests or responses to ensure asynchronous operations are completed before assertions
- Uses simple network mocking or routing to stub out backend calls for happy‑path or error‑path scenarios when given patterns to follow
- Understands how to assert on API responses in UI tests when necessary, such as checking status codes or basic payload fields
- Recognizes when a scenario is better tested via direct API tests vs through the UI and can discuss this with more senior engineers
- Knows that network mocking can improve test speed and determinism but may reduce end‑to‑end realism, and can work within guidelines set by the team", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Debugging and Troubleshooting Playwright Tests", "scope_included": "- Uses Playwright’s debug modes and tools such as headed mode, slow motion and debug log output to understand failing tests
- Uses Playwright Inspector or UI mode to step through actions, inspect locators and refine selectors during test development
- Reads assertion and timeout error messages and stack traces to identify the failing step and probable cause
- Uses screenshots and page snapshots captured on failure to diagnose UI or layout‑related issues
- Introduces temporary logging or console output in tests to understand complex flows or data‑dependent behavior
- Knows how to re‑run a single failing test or a small subset of tests locally to reproduce CI failures
- Can differentiate between an application bug and a test bug and communicates findings clearly to the team
- Understands common anti‑patterns that lead to flaky tests (such as relying on implicit timing, using unstable selectors, test inter‑dependencies) and can fix basic occurrences", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Quality, Maintainability and Best Practices", "scope_included": "- Writes tests that are readable and understandable by other team members, with clear naming for test files, suites and test cases
- Avoids duplicating logic and locators across many tests by introducing simple helper functions or lightweight abstractions
- Applies consistent coding style and structure in line with team conventions for Playwright tests
- Keeps tests focused on business‑relevant behavior rather than low‑value UI details that change frequently
- Uses page object style or similar patterns at a basic level when such patterns are already established in the codebase
- Understands the importance of fast feedback and aims to keep tests targeted and efficient rather than creating overly long flows in a single test
- Considers test coverage when adding or modifying features and can identify missing UI scenarios that should be automated
- Reviews and updates existing tests when application behavior changes, ensuring tests evolve with the product", "is_non_core_section": false}, {"name": "Project Setup and Configuration", "scope_included": "- Understands the structure of a typical Playwright project: configuration file, tests directory, fixtures, helpers and related assets
- Can install Playwright and its browsers using the official tooling and keep them updated under guidance
- Can read and make basic changes to the Playwright configuration: test directory, timeout values, base URL, retries, reporter selection, number of workers
- Can configure simple projects or browser profiles for different environments or browsers within the configuration file when provided with patterns
- Uses environment variables or configuration options to avoid hard‑coding environment‑specific values in tests
- Can set up basic scripts in the package or build configuration to run different test suites (such as smoke tests vs full regression)
- Understands how to tag or filter tests using built‑in mechanisms (such as annotations or grep filters) and can run subsets of tests based on tags or name patterns
- Participates in maintaining the test project structure so that it remains navigable and logical as it grows", "is_non_core_section": true}, {"name": "CLI, Tooling and Reporting", "scope_included": "- Uses the Playwright CLI to run tests locally with common options such as headed mode, browser selection, project selection, retries and debug flags
- Understands and uses the built‑in HTML report to inspect test results, failures, screenshots and logs
- Knows how to configure and open traces or recordings when they are enabled in the project to investigate complex failures
- Uses command‑line filtering to quickly run a single test file, suite or test case during development
- Understands basic exit codes and how they indicate success or failure in local and CI runs
- Knows how to generate and view test artifacts such as screenshots, videos and traces when configured by the team
- Can work with simple integrations between Playwright and other tools such as package managers, task runners or basic CI pipelines at a usage level
- Understands how test reports fit into the team’s quality workflow and uses them to prioritize debugging efforts", "is_non_core_section": true}, {"name": "Collaboration, Documentation and Workflow", "scope_included": "- Participates in code reviews for Playwright tests, providing and receiving feedback on test design, readability and reliability
- Documents new or changed tests with brief comments where the intent is not immediately clear from the code
- Follows team conventions for directory structure, naming, tagging and configuration for Playwright tests
- Collaborates with developers, testers and product owners to clarify expected behavior before or while writing tests
- Communicates clearly about test failures, including steps to reproduce, environment details and whether the issue is in the test or the application
- Contributes to shared documentation on how to run tests locally, how to debug common failures and how to add new tests to the suite
- Aligns with release and CI processes by understanding when and how Playwright tests are executed within pipelines
- Escalates recurring test issues (such as frequent flakes in specific areas) to more senior engineers and participates in their resolution", "is_non_core_section": true}]}
